ThingDef.label: AK-47 Type 2
ThingDef.description:
The original AK-47 had a problem nobody wanted to admit.
The Type 1's stamped sheet-metal receiver — pressed and folded from flat steel — was a compromise born from manufacturing urgency. Get the rifle into production fast. Get it into soldiers' hands. Deal with the engineering problems later.
Four years of field reports described the same failure mode: the receiver cracked under sustained automatic fire, particularly in cold weather when the metal became brittle. It didn't happen immediately. It happened after thousands of rounds, at the end of a service life the weapon was supposed to survive.
The Type 2, introduced in 1951, replaced it entirely. A milled receiver — machined from a solid billet of steel by skilled machinists working to tight tolerances. Not pressed, not folded. Cut. The result was a receiver that didn't crack, didn't flex under fire, and didn't develop the hairline fractures that had been quietly retiring Type 1s ahead of schedule.
The cost was weight. The milled receiver added nearly half a kilogram. It also added time — milling required more skilled labor and more factory hours than stamping. The Soviet military accepted both penalties because the alternative was a rifle that wore out.
It fires the same 7.62x39mm round as the AK-47. The action is identical. The barrel is identical. The accuracy, the rate of fire, the handling — identical. What changed is invisible until the ten-thousandth round, when the Type 1 receiver starts to show its age and the Type 2 does not.
The Krasnaya Zvezda filed it immediately after the original AK-47 in its archive — the correction following the achievement. The weapon that made the legend last long enough to matter.
~wololooo
